---
title: "03_homework_193DS"
author: "Ian Del Tredici"
date: "03-05-2026"
format: html
---

[Github repo](https://github.com/iandeltredici/ENVS-193DS_homework-03.git)

```{r}
#| label: read in packages and data
#| message: false
library(tidyverse) #load in packages
library(here)
library(janitor)
library(readxl)
salinity <- read.csv( #read in pickleweed data using here function
  here("data","salinity-pickleweed.csv"))
reading_data <- read.csv( #read in personal data using here function
  here("data", "reading_data_envs-193DS.csv")
)
```

# Part 2 Problems
## Problem 1. Slough soil salinity

### An appropriate test
Both a Spearman's correlation and a Pearson's correlation test could determine the relationship strength between salinity, a continuous predictor variable, and California pickleweed biomass, a continuous response variable. Pearson's correlation is a parametric test, meaning is assumes normality and uses the actual measurements of continuous data to show the strength and direction of a linear relationship. Meanwhile, Spearman's correlation uses ranks instead of the actual values to determine a monotonic relationship between variables, making it more suitable for non-normal distributions.
